steps.title: What the cultural explanation requires
steps.content: Courel argues that pottery differences between Volga (terrestrial) and Don (aquatic) sites reflect cultural factors, not food availability. For this to hold, both regions must have had similar food resources — otherwise the pottery difference could simply track what food was actually available.

steps.title: Eliminate three, confirm one
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'Across the Volga and Don basins, people had broadly similar access to the same terrestrial and aquatic animal resources.': Similar resources in both regions means the pottery difference cannot be explained by food availability alone — which is exactly what Courel's cultural explanation needs. ✓
  • Choice B — 'In both the Volga and Don basins, most sites appear to have been seasonal fishing and hunting encampments rather than year-round settlements.': Settlement patterns are unrelated to whether food availability or culture drove pottery use. ✗
  • Choice C — 'There were many more bodies of water in the Don basin than in the Volga basin.': More water in the Don basin would suggest more aquatic resources there, which would support a food-availability explanation instead of a cultural one. ✗
  • Choice D — 'The people of the Volga basin acquired pottery techniques from the people of the Don basin.': Shared technique origin doesn't explain why the two regions used pottery for different food types. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
